coredata.dc:title: Functionalization of PET with carbon dots as copolymerizable flame retardants for the excellent smoke suppressants and mechanical properties
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Weiwen
Dong, Zhenfeng
Zhang, Anying
Ma, Tianyi
Hu, Qiang
Wei, Jianfei
Wang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: Based on the excellent flame retardant performance, environment-friendly and abundant surface functional groups, Carbon Dots (CDs) were adopted as copolymerizable flame retardants for Poly(ethylene terephthalate)(PET) for the first time. The CDs with gelatin as precursor (gCDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method and copolymerized with pure terephthalic acid (PTA) and ethylene glycol(EG) to prepare flame retardant copolyester (gCDs-PET) based on the hydroxyl and amine groups on the surface of gCDs by in situ polymerization. It is demonstrated that gCDs-PET had showed better smoke suppressant and higher flame retardancy than pristine PET. The limiting oxygen index (LOI) of gCDs-PET composites achieved to 29% with the addition of 2.0 wt% gCDs. What is more, cone calorimeter tests showed that the peak value of heat release rate(pk-HRR) greatly decreased 42.66% and the total smoke production (TSP) reduced 62.64%. Besides, the flame-retardant mechanism disclosed that gCDs catalyzes the thermal decomposition of PET to form a shielding carbon layer, which handicap the release of combustible materials to the outside and expand the as-formed char, so that interrupt the combustion reaction. Furthermore, mechanical properties and spinnability of gCDs-PET was evaluated. It was demonstrated that the addition of gCDs into PET could enhance the mechanical strength and gCDs-PET could be made to fibers with excellent spinnability.

head.ce:title: Functionalization of PET with carbon dots as copolymerizable flame retardants for the excellent smoke suppressants and mechanical properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Based on the excellent flame retardant performance, environment-friendly and abundant surface functional groups, Carbon Dots (CDs) were adopted as copolymerizable flame retardants for Poly(ethylene terephthalate)(PET) for the first time. The CDs with gelatin as precursor (gCDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method and copolymerized with pure terephthalic acid (PTA) and ethylene glycol(EG) to prepare flame retardant copolyester (gCDs-PET) based on the hydroxyl and amine groups on the surface of gCDs by in situ polymerization. It is demonstrated that gCDs-PET had showed better smoke suppressant and higher flame retardancy than pristine PET. The limiting oxygen index (LOI) of gCDs-PET composites achieved to 29% with the addition of 2.0 wt% gCDs. What is more, cone calorimeter tests showed that the peak value of heat release rate(pk-HRR) greatly decreased 42.66% and the total smoke production (TSP) reduced 62.64%. Besides, the flame-retardant mechanism disclosed that gCDs catalyzes the thermal decomposition of PET to form a shielding carbon layer, which handicap the release of combustible materials to the outside and expand the as-formed char, so that interrupt the combustion reaction. Furthermore, mechanical properties and spinnability of gCDs-PET was evaluated. It was demonstrated that the addition of gCDs into PET could enhance the mechanical strength and gCDs-PET could be made to fibers with excellent spinnability.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most important thermoplastic polymers, which is widely used as engineering plastic, PET have been using as engineering plastic, films and fibers due to its excellent mechanical properties widely, wear resistance, chemical resistance and spinnability [1–4]. However, the LOI of PET is only 21% [5–7], with a large amount of smoke and toxic gasses will be released when burning, which belongs to flammable material. Therefore, it is of great significance to improve the flame retardant performance of PET.

In recent years, the research on flame retardant modification of PET has made great progress, including copolymer phosphorous flame retardant, blend inorganic particles and post-finishing of PET product [2,8]. With the rapid development of nanotechnology, nanomaterials have been gradually applied in the field of flame retardation [9]. It is worth noting that the carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes [10–14], graphene [15], carbon black [16,17] and carbon microspheres [18–21], have been used in the flame retardant modification of polymers due to the obvious predominance in respect of low toxicity, environment-friendly, and facile preparation. It is proved that carbon nanomaterials can significantly reduce the heat and smoke release of polymer materials. Carbon nanotubes can form a cross-linking network during polymer combustion, promote the formation of carbonaceous layer and increase the strength of the carbonaceous layer, thus blocking the heat transfer and gas diffusion [12]. The lamellar structure of graphene can not only play a role of barrier but also facilitate the formation of dense carbonaceous layers during polymer combustion. In addition, catalyzing charring of polymer itself to form a protective carbonaceous layer based on carbon black has been proven to be an ideal flame retardant modification method due to its unique advantages, such as environment-friendly, low filler loading, high carbon yield and so on [22]. However, the flame retardant efficiency is limited when carbon nanomaterials are used alone for flame retardant modification of polymers. What is more, the compatibility of carbon nanomaterials with matrix is poor, and it is difficult to achieve good dispersed in the matrix and easy to agglomerate in the process of flame retardant modification, which affects the mechanical properties of matrix [5].

In this paper, gelatin-based Carbon Dots (gCDs), zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, were firstly adopted as copolymer flame retardants for PET in order to achieve excellent smoke suppressants and enhance the mechanical properties. The gCDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method from gelatin, which is a natural polymer with good pharmacological characteristics (such as excellent biocompatibility and degradability to nontoxic products) and low cost. The chemical structure and morphology of gCDs was characterized through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and Transmission electron microscope (TEM). It was found that gCDs consist of carbon-based core and reactive functional groups (-OH and –NH2) on the surface. The carbon-based core of gCDs make it an excellent flame retardant, while the reactive functional groups on the surface of gCDs can increase its compatibility with the polymer matrix. What is more, gCDs can be adopted as structural unit to copolymerize into macromolecular chains of PET due to the reactive functional groups, improving its dispersion in the matrix and reducing the possibility of agglomeration for gCDs by reaction enhancement [23]. As a result, gCDs copolymerize with terephthalic acid and ethylene glycol to prepare gCDs-PET. Then, crystallization and thermal properties of gCDs-PET composites were evaluated by differential scanning calorimeter (DSC), while fire performance and mechanical properties of flame retardant copolymer were investigated in terms of limiting oxygen index (LOI), vertical burning tests (UL-94), cone calorimeter tests (CONE) and mechanical analysis. Besides, the thermal stabilities of PET and gCDs-PET were assessed by thermogravimetric analyses (TGA), and the flame-retardant mechanism of gCDs-PET was studied through Raman, thermogravimetry-infrared spectrometer (TG-IR) and pyrolysis gas chromatograph-mass spectrometer (Py-GC-MS). Aftesr all, the spinning property of gCDs-PET composites was explored by single screw melt spinning machine.

Gelatin(GA, analytical pure) was provided by Aladdin Reagents Co., Ltd(Shanghai, China). Pure terephthalic acid (PTA, industrial grade) and Ethylene glycol (EG, industrial grade) were supplied by Sinopec Group (Tianjin, China). Triphenyl phosphite (TPI, analytical pure), Diantimony trioxide (Sb2O3, analytical pure) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) .HyMax 1010 (1010, industrial grade) was provided by Langyi Functional Materials Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).All chemicals were used directly without further purification.

In a typical experiment, gCDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis. Firstly, GA (1 g) and water (20 mL) was added into a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined hydrothermal reaction autoclave and allowed to age for 24 h at 240C in blast drying oven. Subsequently, the resulting product cooled to room temperature was filtered with cylinder filtration membrane filter (0.22 μm), and then it was freeze-dried. Finally, the brown powdery product was called gelatin-based Carbon Dots (gCDs). The entire preparation process of gCDs was clearly illustrated in Fig. S1.

gCDs-PET was prepared by in situ polymerization. First of all, powdered raw materials-PTA was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 120 °C for 12 h before being used. Then, PTA, EG and gCDs were mixed in polymerization reactor according to the formulation shown in Table 1 and dispersed evenly by impeller for 5 min to use. Subsequently, the mixture was esterified for 2 h at 240 °C. Next, TPI, HyMax 1010 and a small amount of EG were added to the polymerization reactor to further esterification at atmospheric pressure for 30 min. In the meantime, it can be judged whether the esterification has been completed by the amount of water produced. According to the mole ratio of raw materials, about 151 ml H2O can be got in our experiences. The above-mentioned product was bis(2-hydroxyethyl)terephthalate (BHET). Finally, polycondensation reaction occurred in high vacuum and gCDs-PET was produced at 280 °C. The entire preparation process for gCDs-PET was clearly illustrated in Scheme 1 .
Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were acquired in the Tecnai G2 F30 (FEI Co., USA), and the accelerating voltage was 300 kV.
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were performed on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Nicolet Instrument Co., USA), which scanned from 4000 to 400cm−1and 64 times.

Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) was conducted on the Bruker 500 NMR (Bruker, USA) Deuterated trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) was selected as the solvent of gCDs-PET, and its solvent peak (=11.6 ppm, 1HNMR) was used as the chemical shift scale.
The fluorescence measurements were performed with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS5 from Techcomp (China) LTD).
Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) images were acquired in the JSM-7500F(JEOL, Iapan)

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on a TG 209F1 thermo-analyzer instrument (NETZSCH-Gerätebau GmbH, GER) under nitrogen atmosphere, at a linear heating rate of 10 °C·min−1 from 30 to 700 °C.
The limiting oxygen index (LOI) test was carried out on a Dynisco oxygen index meter (DYNISCO, USA) according to ASTM D2863-2008, and the sample size is 100.0 × 6.5 × 3.0 mm3.

The vertical burning (UL-94) test was conducted on the CZF-3 type horizontal vertical burning tester (Nanjing Jiangning Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., China) according to ASTM D3801, and the sample size was 130 × 13 × 3 mm3 .

The cone calorimetry test (CONE) was conducted according to ISO 5660–1using the Fire Testing Technology (FTT) standard cone calorimeter (FTTL, UK) under an external heat flux of 50 kW/m2, and the size of the sample was 100.0 × 100.0 × 3.0 mm3.

The Differential Scanning Calorimeter (DSC) test were conducted on the DSC Q2000 (TA, USA) under nitrogen atmosphere, and the sample weight was 5–9 mg, at a linear heating rate of 10 °C·min−1 from 30 to 300 °C and cooling rate of 10 °C·min−1 from 300 to 30 °C.

Thermogravimetric (TG) and thermogravimetric-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (TG-FTIR) curves were obtained by TGA5500 thermogravimetric analyzer (TA, USA) coupled with iS50 FTIR spectrophotometer (Nicolet, USA) from room temperature to 800 °C with a linear heating rate of 20 °C·min−1 under the nitrogen flow of 100 mL·min−1.

Raman test, used to characterize the graphitization degree of residual carbon, was carried out on the Raman microscope (Renishaw, UK). The sample was fully dried before test, and the laser wavelength is 325 nm.

Tensile was tested with a constant rate of 2 mm·min−1 according to GB/T 1040-92 on INSTRON 5966 electronic universal testing machine (The Northwest Research Institute of Chemical Industry Co., Ltd., China). Herein, the specimens for tensile tests were dumbbell-shaped, and the sample size was 30.00 × 4.00 × 2.00 mm3. The tests for each sample were duplicated up to five times.

Py-GC-MS test was carried on PET and gCDs-PET to identity the thermal degradation/pyrolysis products. Firstly, the sample in the crucible was pyrogenic decomposed in the EGA/PY-3030 pyrolyzer (Frontier, Japan) by heating from ambient to 700 °C (at 435 °C) for 300 s. Afterwards, the products were carried through helium to the DB-5MS capillary column (Agilent, USA) wherein the temperature of the capillary column (30.00 × 0.25 × 0.25 m3) of GC was firstly hold at 50 °C for 2 min and then increased to 250 °C at a heating rate of 20 °C·min−1 and kept at 250 °C for 20 min. Besides, MS indicator was operated in the electron impact mode at the electron energy of 70 eV, and the scanning range of 20–650 m/z.

Single yarn strength was tested on HD021N electronic single yarn strength meter (Nantong Hongda Experimental Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The tensile rate of 500 mm/min and sample clamping distance was setted100 mm.

gCDs are prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis with gelatin as precursor and characterized by TEM, 1HNMR, FTIR and FS5. Fig. 1 a is the TEM imagine of the gCDs. It is can be seen that gCDs are spherical structures with a narrow size distribution in the range from 4.3 to 8.8 nm. The average size of gCDs is 6.2 nm. Fig. 1b shows that the fluorescence emission peak of the gCDs shifts to longer wavelength as the excitation wavelengths increase from 280 to 400 nm. The strongest fluorescence emission center at 400 nm is observed in the emission spectra when the excitation wavelength is 320 nm. Fig. 1c shows the FTIR of the gCDs. The peak at 3400 cm−1 indicates the presence of the typical stretching vibration peak of O-H, and the absorption bonds at 1080 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CO. The peak at 610 cm−1 is caused by bending vibration of COH. The existence of C = O stretching vibration peak at 1590 cm−1 indicates the presence of -OH and other oxygen-containing functional groups in the surface of gCDs [5,24]. Additionally, broad spectra in the range of 3040–3260 cm−1and one peak at 1400 cm−1 are specific for the vibration and deformation bands of NH, which confirms the presence of amino-containing functional groups. Fig. 1d shows the 1H NMR spectrum of the gCDs. The main characteristic resonances at δ=7.33 ppm indicates the presence of the amide group. The peaks at δ=2.34 and δ=2.54 ppm presents the different types of fatty alcohol in the gCDs. The peaks at δ=3.61–3.22 and δ=0.86 ppm indicate the presence of -NHCH2- and -CH3 group respectively [25]. Hence, the presence of -OH and -NH2 on the surface of gCDs can be speculated from the 1H NMR spectrum, which is consistent with the result of FTIR. According to the above discussion, the structure of gCD is demonstrated in Fig. 1e.

The structure and morphology of gCDs-PET are characterized by FTIR, 1HNMR and SEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . FTIR spectra is employed to confirm the occurrence of copolymerization of the co-monomers gCDs into PET chains. For gCDs-PET, all the typical peaks of PET still exist indicate gCDs is copolymerized with PET in the form of ester group. The apparent peak at 2831 cm−1 is correspond to the stretching vibration of the -CH3 groups of gCDs components [26]. Moreover, some new absorption peaks appear. The peaks from 437 to 500 cm−1 are assigned to the weaker characteristic absorption of the -C(=O)-N(-H)-C, which indicates successful condensation reaction between gCDs and PET, which enlarge figure can be seen Fig. S2. In addition, 1H-NMR of PET and gCDs-PET are shown in Fig. 2a. It is seen that the signal peak at 8 ppm belongs to the proton of benzene ring of PET counterpoint H, and hydrogen atoms of the methylene group bond to oxygen (-CH2-O-) are located at δ= 4.7 ppm. It can be seen from Fig. 2b that the protons of -NH2 and -OH are also detected at δ=0.5 ppm and δ=0.7 ppm, respectively, which confirms that gCDs-PET is synthesized successfully. In order to more intuitively judge the dispersion state of gCDs in the PET matrix, the freeze-fractured surfaces of the PET and gCDs-PET are observed by employing SEM, as shown in Fig. S3a and b. It can be seen that gCDs was evenly dispersed in PET matrix, which shows that the compatibility of gCDs in the PET matrix is extremely great because existing a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of gCDs.

Based on the SEM, FTIR and NMR analysis, some useful surmises may be put forward. gCDs has excellent dispersibility in PET matrix, and the -OH and -NH2 on the surface of gCDs are directly connected to ester group structure, which will be given full play to the in-situ catalytic charring effect of gCDs. The gCDs-PET schematic is illustrated in Fig. 2d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystallization and thermal properties of gCDs-PET
ce:section.ce:para: The intrinsic viscosity of PET and gCDs-PET composites can be measured by using Ubbelohde viscometer, and the results can be found in Table 2 . The intrinsic viscosity and the molecular weight of PET are 0.72 dL/g and 20,312. Comparing with PET, as the amount of gCDs increases, the intrinsic viscosity and the molecular weight of gCDs-PET first increases and then decreases, because the active functional groups on the surface of gCDs promote molecular chain growth and partial cross-linking reaction occurred between gCDs and PET monomer contributing to the increase of polyester intermolecular forces. When the amount of gCDs reaches 3.0%, the intrinsic viscosity and molecular weight present great downward trend. The reason is that excessive gCDs will break the ratio of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in the PET reaction system.

The thermal behavior of gCDs-PET composites is studied using the DSC test [27]. Fig. 3 a demonstrates the DSC melting curves of gCDs-PET composites and Fig. 3b indicates the DSC curves of gCDs-PET cooling scan. The results of Fig. 3 are classified in Table 3 .

The glass transition temperature (Tg) is mainly determined by the flexibility of the molecular chain and the force between the molecules. The chain moves easier for the molecular with the lower Tg. As the amount of gCDs increases, the Tg increases first and then decreases. When the addition amount of gCDs reaches 1%, Tg arrive at 74 °C. This is because gCDs are copolymerized with PET polymer molecular chain, which increases steric hindrance and reduces the flexibility of the molecular chain. At the same time, the force between the molecular chains increases and the internal slight cross-linked structure is formed, which leads to an increase in Tg. What is more, the cold crystallization temperature (Tcc) increases and the thermal crystallization temperature (Tmc) decreases when the addition amount of gCDs increases. This is because the addition of gCDs reduces the degree of ordering of the copolyester segments, which contributes to increases Tcc and decreases Tmc. When the addition of gCDs reaches 3%, its temperature arrives at 128 °C. As regard to Tmc, when the addition of gCDs gets to 2% and 3%, its decrease to 197 °C, which because the embedding of gCDs destroys the structure of PET macromolecular chains, and the interaction between molecular chains increases, making crystallization difficultly. In the meanwhile, it is proved that gCDs is grafted into PET molecular chain. When the addition amount of gCDs reaches 2% and 3%, the peak width becomes narrower. This is because the internal cross-linked structure is formed and the crystallization process time is shortened. ΔTmc is the degree of undercooling, which is defined as the difference between Tm and Tmc in Table 3 [28,29], the smaller value of ΔTmc is, the easier it is to crystallize, and the faster the crystallization rate. From Table 3 can be seen that with contents of gCDs increases, the value of ΔTmc exists increasing difference. In the meantime, ΔTmc is also an important parameter to measure the spinnability of polymer slices, the greater ΔTmc with the better the spinnability. Hence, gCDs-PET composite has excellent spinnability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fire retardancy of PET and gCDs-PET
ce:section.ce:para: The flammability properties of the gCDs-PET composite are assessed by UL-94 and LOI tests, and the results are presented in Table 4 . Pure PET is found combustible, with an LOI value of 21% and no classification in the UL-94 rating. According to the results of the UL-94 rating, it is demonstrated that the V-2 rating is achieved with total burning time of 55.4 s, when only 0.5% gCDs is added. The total burning time of gCDs-PET further decreases to 42.62 s with the further increases of gCDs contents, and the UL-94 rating is still V-2 due to the absorbent cotton is ignited. What is more, as the addition amount of gCDs increases, it can be found that the LOI value of the gCDs-PET composite shows improvement trend. The LOI value increases to 29% with the addition amount of gCDs is 2.0%. However, the LOI value declines with the further increase of gCDs content. Hence, gCDs is applicable as the flame retardant for PET and the suitable addition content of gCDs is 2.0%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cone test of PET and gCDs-PET
ce:section.ce:para: To further assess the flame-retardant properties of the PET composites, the combustion heat is quantitatively analyzed by the cone method. The cone method can accurately measure vast of combustion parameters [30,31]. The heat release rate (HRR) and peak HRR (pk-HRR) are two important fire characteristic parameters of materials, whose higher values mean higher fire risk of gCDs-PET composite [32]. Fig. 4 a indicates that the addition of gCDs could reduce HRR of PET. The HRR of the gCDs-PET composite trend to decreases when the gCDs addition increase. Comparing with pristine PET the pk-HRR reduced by 42.66% when the content of gCDs is 3.0%. The cause might be that a residual protective char layer is formed when the gCDs-PET composite is combusted. Another reason is likely to that lowering the release of combustible volatiles and reducing the pk-HRR. In addition, it can be found that the TTI is shortened when the addition of gCDs is small (0.5%, 1.0%), while the TTI is longed when the addition of gCDs is 2.0% and 3.0%. TTI is mainly affected by the thermal conductivity and heat absorption of matrix. The good thermal conductivity and the awful heat absorption is benefit for the increase of TTI. The addition of gCDs is good to the heat absorption. As a result, the addition of gCDs induce an acceleration of ignition when the small content of gCDs. However, when the content of gCDs is more than 1%, a heat conduction network is formed and the TTI of gCDs-PET is delayed. THR refers to the total amount of heat released by the material from ignition to flame extinguishment under the preset intensity of incident heat flow. It can be seen from Fig. 4b that the THR of gCDs-PET composites keep decline trend, when the gCDs content is increase. Comparing with pristine PET, the THR decline 12% with the gCDs content are 2% and 3%, which means gCDs catalyzes carbide to form effective carbon layer and the specific values can be seen at Table 5 . Comparing with traditional test methods, the CONE also can measure the dynamic generation of smoke during combustion. The smoke production rate (SPR) and total smoke production (TSP) curves are shown in Fig. 4 and the corresponding specific parameters are listed in Table 5. The SPR and TSP curves in Fig. 4 shows that reducing the smoke production rate and total smoke production during the combustion process of PET with the addition of gCDs. As the content of gCDs rose, the SPR, TSP, CO and CO2 release rate (Fig. S4) of the gCDs-PET composites all declined. These results suggest that the smoke and gas release amounts decrease. The values of TSP, CO and CO2 yield are recorded as 7.26 m2, 0.047 kg/kg, and 1.70 kg/kg, when the content of gCDs is 2.0%. The smoke parameter (SP, which is defined as the proportion of the average of specific extinction area to pk-HRR) is introduced to assess the smoke generating capacity of gCDs-PET composite during the combustion process. The smaller value indicates a lower ability to produce smoke [30,33]. The Table 5 shows that at gCDs amounts of 0.5%, 1%, 2%, and 3%, the SP values of the gCDs-PET composites decreased by 36.27%, 65.26%, 88.94%, and 74.80% compared with pure PET. The reason for the decline of the smoke capacity of gCDs-PET composite may be related to two factors [34]. Firstly, gCDs have effective free radical(H·,·OH) scavenging activity and antioxidant activity during combustion [35,36]. Secondly, the gCDs can effectively catalyze the PET matrix carbonization, which forms an effective shielding carbon layer.

To further explore flame retardant mechanism according to the effective heat combustion (EHC) and residue amount (Table 5). EHC stands for the ratio of the measured heat release rate to the mass loss rate at a certain time, which reflects the combustion degree of volatile gas in the gas-phase flame [37]. According to the data in Table 5, the mean of effective heat combustion (MEHC) decreases with the increase of the addition of gCDs. What is more, the residue amount is remarkably increased after gCDs was introduced into the PET matrix, which means gCDs catalyzes the carbonization of PET matrix and prevents heat release, thus reducing the release of thermal decomposition fragments of gas. Overall data illustrates the achievement of the double effect of flame retardant and smoke suppression.

To explore the specific flame-retardant mode, the results are quantified according to Eqs. (1)–(3) [30], and the results are gathered in Table 6 . The barrier and protective, flame inhibition and char effects are three flame-retardant modes affecting the gCDs-PET composite. The proportion of barrier and protective, flame inhibition, and char effects of the gCDs-PET composite at 2% are estimated to be 31.05%, 14.22%, and 9.05% respectively. Hence, the barrier effect of the gCDs for the PET is demonstrated. The barrier effect of gCDs-PET is strengthened due to catalyzed carbonization by gCDs, and the resulting char layer could reduce O2 spread to the carbon layer internal and combustible debris diffuses from the inside of the char layer to the outside to improve the barrier properties. The flame inhibition effect was mainly induced during the gasification and decomposition of gCDs-PET, where the released NH3 diluted the O2 concentration. Overall, the two aspects synthetically achieved a flame retardant and smoke suppression.

In addition, obtaining the above intuitive parameters from the test. The hazard significance of combustion can also be evaluated by calculating the relationship between the parameters. The calculation of these parameters, including fire growth index (FGI), flame performance index (FPI) has a certain guiding significance for exploring the flame retardant mechanism. Fire growth index (FGI) is an important parameter used to evaluate the thermal capacity of materials [34]. It is defined as the ratio of PHRR to the time reaching pk-HRR (tpk-HRR).Higher FGI values mean faster fire spread and easier burn-out of a given material. The results reveal that the FGI value of PET is the highest, and the FGI values for gCDs-PET composites are comparably lower. The addition of gCDs improves the thermal stability of PET and the fire spreading speed of PET get to lower obviously. The fire safety performance index (FPI) is calculated according to the conical calorimetric data [21]. The higher value is, the more secure it is. Comparing with PET, the FPI value of 2.0-gCDs-PET and 3.0-gCDs-PET increased by 24.42%, which means the PET matrix get to highest fire safety with the addition of gCDs reaches 2% or 3%.

The TGA and DTG curves of PET, gCDs and its copolymer are demonstrated in Fig. 5 , and some typical data are listed in Table 7 . As can be seen in Fig. 5 and Table 7, there is clear turning point in the TGA curves of gCDs at 120 °C. Its weight loss, the main reason is that the desorption of absorbed water. The second turning point in the TGA curves of gCDs at 230 °C, which including decomposition of partial amorphous carbons and separation of impurities. starts from about 240 °C and still decline at 405 °C. Its weight loss including decomposition of functional groups and further carbonization weightlessness. The last turning point at 680 °C and then residual weight further decreased, maybe that's because the gCDs was completely carbonized contributed lose the character of carbon Dots to become graphite carbon. The temperature at maximum weight loss rate (Tmax) of gCDs is 305 °C, and its residual weight at 700 °C is 12.88%.

Comparing with neat PET, the T5wt% of gCDs-PET has obvious increase while the Tmax are not affected, which implies that the addition of gCDs is beneficial for the thermal stability and the reason that gCDs was grafted on the PET chains and formed internal crosslinking in PET matrix. Moreover, the internal crosslinking structure makes better heat transfer of gCDs-PET composites than neat PET matrix. Therefore, the initial decomposition temperature of gCDs-PET composite above neat PET.

To further study the synergetic flame retardant of components, TGA curves of experiment and theory are presented in Fig. 5, and the theoretical curves are gained according to the following formula.

where X and Y represent neat PET and flame retardant, respectively, and Mtheo.(T) X + Y is the theoretical mass of PET composites. Mexp.(T)X is the experimental mass of neat PET, and Mexp(T)Y is the experimental mass of flame retardant [38–41]. As can be seen in Fig. 5c, the experimental curves of 2.0-gCDs-PET are similar to the theoretical curves, but the experimental residual weight at 700 °C (CR700 °C exp.) are higher than the theoretical residual weight at 700 °C (CR700 °C cal.), which means that gCDs has the charring effect on PET to some extent. The results imply that gCDs can improve thermal stability as well as promote the charring of PET. The high yield of residual char at high temperature of gCDs-PET suggests that gCDs is an efficient char agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Composition, structure and micromorphology of the char layer
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the flame retardant mechanism of gCDs in the PET matrix, the morphology and structure of char layer of PET and gCDs-PET composites after cone testing are investigated and shown in Fig. 6 . Complete combustion of pristine PET gives carbonaceous layer without outstanding swelling and volume gain (Fig. 6a1). Adding gCDs initiates intumescence, which explains the lower pk-HRR, THR, SPR, and TSP of the gCDs-PET in comparison with PET. When the addition amount of gCDs is 2.0% and 3.0%, the height of the char layer reached about 3 cm. These char layer could be served as a barrier for the beneath polymeric substrate during the burning and prevent the fuel volatiles. What is more, a porous, brittle, and flaky char could be observed for pristine PET, while the continuous and compact char layer is formed for gCDs-PET, which contributes to prevent further decomposition of PET matrix significantly. Morphology of char layer is an important element to evaluate the quality of protective barrier in the prevention the mass transfer and the attack of oxygen to combustion zone. Comparing with neat PET, from the Fig. 6(b3, c3, d3 and e3) can be seen, the number of holes on the surface of the same area char layer decreased with the increase of the amount of gCDs, and the hole area decreased gradually.

The chemical structure of residual char of PET composites after cone test was further examined by FTIR. Fig. 7 shows absorption bands at 3053,1600 and 751 cm−1 for the residual char of PET, which are assigned respectively to the -CH2-,C = C and -Ar of PET main chain [42–44]. Comparing with neat PET, the char structure of gCDs-PET is also shown in Fig. 7.The increase for the strength of the stretching vibration peak at 1600 cm−1 implies an incomplete decomposition of the gCDs-PET. A new stretching vibration peak appears at 1080 cm−1is correspond to COC [42], which demonstrate that gCDs-PET composites are catalytical carbonized so that forming a continuous and compact carbon structure during combustion. The NH stretching vibration peak at 3352 cm−1 means a compound containing N appears in the condense phase product of gCDs-PET.

To quantitatively assess graphitization degree and regularity degree of residual carbon [45], the Raman spectra of char residue for PET and gCDs-PET are obtained and shown in Fig. 8 . Two important string peaks can be observed at 1350 cm−1 (D band) and 1580 cm−1 (G band). Through the peak processing D band and G band high peak and got to peak height ratio (ID/IG) to express the degree of graphitization. Smaller value of ID/IG indicates a higher density of point defects. The ID/IG of char residue for neat PET is 0.71. However, the ID/IG of char residue for 2.0-gCDs-PET is 0.53, which means the graphitic degree of gCDs-PET was improved and the shielding effect against heat and smoke is stronger during combustion.

To further study the thermal decomposition mechanism of gCDs-PET composites and the combustion behavior, the thermal degradation process and thermal meteorological products of each sample are analyzed by TG-IR combined technology [46]. 3D TG-IR spectra of gas-phase for the thermal decomposition of PET and gCDs-PET are shown in Fig. 9 . As shown in Fig. 9, there are 3-stage evolutions for CO2 and C = O in carbonyl moieties like aldehyde or carboxylic acid are detected range from room temperature to 800 °C [46,47]. By correlating this data with TGA results, it is evident that the first stage is corresponds to T5%, second stage to oxidation of decomposition products, while the final stage is a result of oxidation of char. Comparing with neat PET, the CO2 release rate of gCDs-PET at 460 °C is slightly higher, while the release rate of carbonyl moieties for gCDs-PET is less than PET, whose reason is that gCDs catalyzes the thermal decomposition of PET. It is worth noting that the CO2 release rate of gCDS-PET increases obviously at 700 °C, and the release rate of carbonyl decreases slightly, which is relate to the change from carbonyl moieties to CO2 due to the addition of gCDs. In other words, gCDs will catalyze the deep degradation of carbonyl moieties to CO2. The results show that the addition of gCDs does not fundamentally change the evolved products in the gas-phase, but influence the product release rate.

Fig. 10 demonstrates the infrared spectra of samples at 300 °C, 430 °C, 500 °C and 600 °C, respectively. It can be seen that PET and gCDs-PET have no obvious absorption peak before 300 °C, indicating that no obvious decomposition reaction occurred before 300 °C. When the temperature arrives at 430 °C, the thermal degradation reaction of sample starts and the infrared spectra of PET and gCDs-PET shows obvious vibration absorption peak. In the FTIR spectrum of PET, 2763cm−1, 2358 cm−1, 2120 cm−1,1760 cm−1and 746 cm−1 are correspond to the structure of alkane, CO2, CO, C = O and benzene ring respectively [47], indicating that PET cleavage is relatively complete and the main evolved products decomposition included benzoic acid, CO2, CO and aromatic esters. Comparing with neat PET, the absorption peaks at 2358 cm−1 becomes sharper, indicating that CO2 release rate increases. The infrared spectra of gas phase products at 500 °C and 600 °C are similar to those at 430 °C. It is worth noting that at 500 °C, the CH absorption peak of the PET at 2763 cm−1 disappears, indicating that the release rate of benzoic acid decreases for gCDs-PET, which is consistent with the cone TSP results.

To explore the thermal degradation mechanism, Py-GC-MS is selected to monitor the product of gaseous phase for the pristine PET and gCDs-PET composite [48]. The thermal decomposition of the PET and gCDs-PET are qualitatively analyzed at 435 °C by Py-GC-MS, and the data are listed in Table 8 . The thermal decomposition process is shown in Fig. 11 .

The main pyrolysis products of the gCDs-PET composites are 44.43% benzoic acid, 40.73% terephthalic acid, 1.73% 4-ethylbenzoic acid, 1.67% 4-vinylbenzoic acid, 1.45% ethane-1,2-diyldibenzoate. Comparing with pure PET, the obvious changes in the content of the pyrolysis products are mainly reflect in the raised amounts of 4-vinylbenzoic acid and terephthalic acid, as well as in the decline amount of benzoic acid, 4-methylbenzoic acid, 3-ethylbenzaldehyde free radical and segments of the PET. Benzoic acid is the main pyrolysis product of PET, and the decline contents indicates that gCDs catalyzes degradation to produce CO2, CO and other gas. At high temperature, the declining in 4-methylbenzoic acid and 3-ethylbenzaldehyde free radical further contributes to the product of small molecular debris, containing benzene ring, which may be the reason for the decrease of TSP at CONE test . Besides, the decline in benzoic acid, ethane-1,2-diyldibenzoate, segments of the PET and ethyl 4-formylbenzoate free radical demonstrated that gCDs could catalyze the degradation of them. In addition, high amounts of formic acid could generate more CO2 and H2O during combustion, which may be the reason for the improving properties of LOI and UL-94 tests and the increased of CO2 release rate of TG-IR. In addition to the above pyrolysis products, about 0.36% of new pyrolysis products is formed, which was long chain olefins and it is the main product of unbound gCDs. The restraint in benzene acid and long chain olefins from deep degradation could explain the improvement in thermal stability of the gCDs-PET composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flame retardant effect of condensed phase and gas phase jointly
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above discussions, the flame-retardant mechanism of gCDs-PET composites was illustrated in Fig. 12 .

Firstly, when the gCDs-PET composites are ignited, TTI is extended, because gCDs has been grafted into PET chains and contributed to form an internal crosslinking so that the heat is uniformly transmitting in the PET matrix. Secondly, with the increase of combustion intensity, the macromolecular chain begins to break and gCDs catalyzes degradation. Then, gCDs catalyzes carbonization to form a stable carbon layer, which prevents the volatile decomposition products from moving out of the gCDs-PET composite in the process of thermal decomposition to form an expansive carbon layer, and interrupt the combustion reaction. Meanwhile, gCDs-PET composites noncombustible gasses are released to dilute the oxygen concentration, and gCDs has effective free radical scavenging activity and antioxidant activity so as to achieve the purpose of flame retardant [8].

The mechanical properties would be ranked as basic requirements for material processing, as well as a magnitude performance requirement of flame retardant materials. gCDs are copolymerized in the PET matrix by in-situ polymerization aiming to improve its flame retardant properties and at the same time enhance its mechanical properties by increasing the matrix crosslinking points. The tensile strength is shown in Fig. 13 , and the specific values of tensile strength, elastic modulus, and elongation at break of gCDs-PET composite materials are gathered in Table 9 . Obviously, the small addition of gCDs causes deterioration in the tensile strength of the PET. When the addition amount of gCDs is 0.5%, the tensile strength of the gCDs-PET composite decreases nearly 7.43%. This decrease is mainly due to break down of the linear molecular structure for PET by the addition of gCDs. When the content of gCDs is added to 2.0%, the tensile strength increases nearly 9.0%. The major reason is that gCDs and PET formed a stable crosslinking structure. Meanwhile, its elongation increases near 45%, indicating that gCDs could effectively transfer bearing stress. Elastic modulus has no obvious change, which means the addition of gCDs has no effect on the rigidity of PET matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of gCDs-PET fibers manufactured by melt spinning process
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the spinnability of gCDS-PET composites, gCDs-PET fiber is manufactured though melt spinning. Firstly, gCDs-PET is dried for 12 h and keep at 140 °C in vacuum drying oven. Secondly, the dried slices are manufactured on the SJ-120 single screw spinning machine to get to primary fiber. The specific process and parameters of melt spinning can be seen from Scheme 2 and Table 10 . Then, the primary fibers are drawn 3.5 times to obtain the gCDs-PET fiber bundle and the mechanical properties of the fibers are shown in Table 11 . As can be seen from the Table 11, 1.0-gCDs-PET had good spinnability with excellent mechanical property. What is more, the SEM images shows that 1.0-gCDs-PET fiber surface was smooth and without agglomeration. It is worth noting that 2.0-gCDs-PET has not spinnability, because gCDs plays a role of internal cross links melt spinning process and crosslinking points were formed in the PET matrix, which makes it hard to pass through the filter hole.

A novel flame retardant (gCDs) is successfully prepared through an environment-friendly hydrothermal process [49]. The hydroxyl and amine groups on the surface of gCDs make it possible that gCDs copolymerize with terephthalic acid and ethylene glycol to prepare flame retardant copolyester (gCDs-PET). After the addition of gCDs into PET by in situ polymerization, the flame retardancy of the PET matrix ameliorates considerably. When the content of gCDs in PET matrix is 2.0%, the UL-94 rating is V-2 and the LOI is 29%. What is more, cone calorimeter tests shows that the peak value of heat release rate greatly decrease from 1144 kW/m2 to 656 kW/m2, and the productions of smoke and toxic gasses including CO and CO2 are also remarkably reduce. Furthermore, the mechanical strength of 2.0-gCDs-PET is enhanced to some extent. tensile strength increases from 50.46 MPa of PET to 54.96 MPa, and elongation at break from 300% to 545%. Then, the flame-retardant mechanism is disclosed that gCDs catalyzes the thermal decomposition of PET to form a shielding carbon layer, which handicap the release of combustible materials to the outside and expand the formed char, so that interrupt the combustion reaction. Meanwhile, noncombustible gasses is released to dilute the oxygen concentration, so as to achieve the purpose of flame retardant. Finally, 1.0-gCDs-PET could be made to fibers with excellent mechanical property.
